title: The 60 Graphics Companies Parable: Why Huang Won't Predict AI's Winners

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
When asked why Nvidia goes out of its way not to pick winners among AI companies, Huang reaches back to the company's founding era with a story that doubles as a warning. In the early days of 3D graphics, there were 60 companies competing. Nvidia survived. But Huang's point isn't about triumph—it's about how unlikely that survival was. "Nvidia would be at the top of that list not to make it," he says. The company's original architecture was "precisely wrong," not just slightly off, but fundamentally unworkable for developers.

This formative experience shapes Nvidia's current stance toward the AI ecosystem. Huang invests in OpenAI, Anthropic, and a wide array of startups not because he's identified which will dominate, but because he's learned that prediction is futile. The company that looks most doomed might be the one that pivots into dominance. The one that looks inevitable might be building on a flawed premise. Humility about outcomes, forged in near-death experience, is now corporate policy.

The practical consequence is a kind of deliberate neutrality. Nvidia allocates engineering support, investment capital, and early hardware access across the AI landscape rather than concentrating resources on perceived frontrunners. When Huang invests in one lab, he invests in others. When he supports one neocloud, he supports its competitors too. The goal is to ensure that the entire ecosystem thrives on the Nvidia stack, regardless of which individual companies succeed.

This approach carries real costs. By not picking winners, Nvidia forgoes the potential upside of deep strategic alignment with whichever lab ultimately dominates. But Huang sees the alternative as far riskier. Concentrating bets would alienate the rest of the ecosystem and undermine the platform neutrality that makes Nvidia's CUDA stack the default choice for developers worldwide. The lesson from those 60 graphics companies isn't just that prediction is hard—it's that the company best positioned to survive is the one that serves everyone.
